data.body:
[G4 child-consistency — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with THIS child (S1 6d56 Faithful, lock-free snapshot materialization core)]

Ran the 7 cross-child interaction modes for this materialization-core child against the WHOLE sibling roster (S2 70c7, S3 fa49, S4 30b3, S5 b7fa, S6 1ba5). All 7 modes clean under the high-confidence / benign-reading filter — empty G4 findings list for this pairing.

Mode-by-mode:
- implicit shared state: S1 is the PRODUCER of the snapshot dir + resolved immutable SHA + the materialize() handle. Consumed coherently: S2 caches the entry ("populated via S1's atomic dir-rename"), S3 re-roots the gate file tools onto the snapshot dir, S4 signs over the resolved SHA (verified_at_sha), S5 surfaces it, S6 documents the credential error + portability knobs. No shared-state value is produced-without-consumer or consumed-without-producer; the producer→consumer chain is consistent.
- conflicting assumptions: S1's "attested fails closed, local still runs" (AC5) matches S3 AC2 (local in-place dirty read) and S6 (documents fail-closed-vs-local-still-runs). S1's faithful read-tree+checkout-index (NOT git archive) is consistent with the parent's "What" and S4's reproducibility claim. No contradiction with any sibling.
- dependency gap: Every obligation S1 hands off lands on a named sibling — descriptive credential error raised here is documented by S6 (S1 "see S6"; S6 AC2 claims it); the materialized dir is consumed by S2/S3; the resolved SHA by S4/S5. The fetch-prerequisite note (uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant) is self-contained: S1 supplies a fallback ("or fetch a containing ref then resolve"), so it is not an unowned upstream. No gap on this child's outputs.
- scope overlap: S1's "Coalesced git fetch origin" (fetch-coalescing of the lock-taking step) is a DISTINCT layer from S2's "single-flight per SHA" (coalescing of materialization for a given SHA). S1 Scope: "Out: the cache/janitor (S2)"; S2 Scope: "Out: materialization itself (S1)" and S2 populates "via S1's atomic dir-rename" — clean producer/cache split, no double-implementation. S1's flock+atomic-mkdir-fallback portability seam vs S2's cross-process flock(LOCK_EX) on locks/<sha> + gc/lock: S1 ESTABLISHES the seam, S2's cross-process locks RIDE on it; no re-ownership of the tar/tmp/flock-fallback sub-clauses. No overlap.
- ordering violation: logical S1→S2/S3→S4→S5, with S6 documenting; all six children carry empty deps uniformly and parent AC8 only requires all-closed + green, so no encoded ordering is violated.
- consumer impact: S1's materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle contract is consumed by S2 (cache population), S3 (re-rooting), S4 (resolved-SHA signing). LFS-pointer detection + defined submodule handling (S1 AC2) feed S4's "this is the code at SHA X" attestation faithfully; no downstream contract is broken by this child's surface.
- residual references: S6 (docs), S2 (cache/janitor), S3 (gate wiring), S4 (signing), REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR, uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant, --filter=blob:none all resolve to real producers/trackers in the roster. No dangling reference.

Note (not a G4 finding on THIS pairing): the standing dependency-gap finding on sibling S4 (30b3) concerns WHO routes the pinned-SHA snapshot handle into src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py's dependency_hashes() / _rehash() / claim-gate freshness re-check — a code seam between S3 and S4. That seam is downstream of S1: S1 correctly PRODUCES the resolved-SHA snapshot handle; the unrouted hand-off is on the consuming (S3/S4) side, not a defect in this materialization-core child. It introduces no inconsistency between S1 and the parent/siblings.

Conclusion: no high-confidence cross-child inconsistency between this materialization-core child and the parent/siblings. Empty G4 findings list for this pairing.
